files.SKILL.md:
# First Principles

Read what is present.
Build what is asked.
Change only what matters.
Prove what you claim.
Land the artifact.
Carry forward only the rule.

## Read what is present

Start with the environment note, the task, the relevant prior records, and the exposed tests.

If prior results, notes, or commits point to the same class of failure, read those before rediscovering it.

Current evidence beats memory.

## Build what is asked

Produce exactly the deliverable the task names, in the place it names, in the form it names.

No adjacent rewrites.
No cleanup detours.
No unrelated test edits.
Preserve names, shapes, and behavior you were not asked to change.

## Change only what matters

Match the repository’s existing style, helpers, types, and structure.

Use the local tool that fits the job.
Do not introduce a new dependency or broader pattern unless the repository already uses it or the task requires it.

A narrow fix is easier to verify than a broad one.

## Prove what you claim

Visible green is necessary, not sufficient.

Run the strongest checks the environment affords and read their output.

At the edge, hold the contract exactly:
zero, one, max, negative if allowed, threshold, exact-equal, first, last.

For long-run code, ask what grows, what races, what clock can lie, and what sibling invariant your patch could break.

A fix that passes the obvious path but fails at the boundary is not done.

## Land the artifact

A description of the work is not the work.

Before you stop, confirm the required artifact exists:
- the branch, if one was required
- the commit, if one was required
- the file, if one was required
- the diff, clean and limited to the task

Do not end on intention.
Do not leave the answer only in notes or an uncommitted working tree.

Stage only what belongs to the task.
Leave out caches, binaries, debug residue, and unrelated churn.

## Carry forward only the rule

If a persistent notebook exists, leave at most one short note after the artifact lands.

Record:
- the class of mistake
- the boundary or invariant that mattered
- the shortcut that failed
- the rule that prevents it next time

Do not dump the whole solution.
Do not invent examples or identifiers you cannot point to in the workspace.

## Tool axioms

Stop reading when the next edit is clear.
After the minimum useful reading, create a real artifact.
Independent reads in parallel; edits and conclusions in order.
If a prior failure already explains this task, start from that evidence instead of re-deriving from scratch.
Read after write.
